title: How to Register Your Address in Japan
summary: After moving to Japan or moving within Japan, you may need to register your address at your city or ward office. This is one of the first important steps for daily life.
keywords: How to Register Your Address in Japan | Japan Life Explained 🐾 Japan Life Explained Articles About FAQ Official resources Sources Disclaimer City Office & ID How to Register Your Address in Japan After moving to Japan or moving within Japan, you may need to register your address at your city or ward office. This is one of the first important steps for daily life. Quick answer Address registration connects your legal address to your Residence Card and local municipality. It can affect health insurance, resident tax, My Number, bank accounts, phone contracts, and official mail. Go to the city or ward office that handles your new address and complete the required moving procedure. Simple diagram Move to a new address new home in Japan → City / ward office submit moving notice → Address is recorded Residence Card and records Why address registration matters Your registered address is used for many everyday systems in Japan. If your address is not registered or is outdated, other procedures may become difficult. Opening or updating a bank account Signing a phone contract Receiving official mail Health insurance and pension procedures Resident tax procedures My Number-related documents School, employer, or immigration-related paperwork Common situations Moving into Japan If you newly arrive in Japan and have an address, you usually register it at the local city or ward office. Moving within the same city or ward You may need to submit a moving notice for the new address within the same municipality. Moving to another city or ward You may need procedures at both the old and new municipalities, such as moving-out and moving-in notices. Leaving Japan You may need to notify the municipality before leaving, especially if ending your residence in Japan. Typical moving procedures Moving in Submit a moving-in notice at the city or ward office for your new address. Moving out If moving to another municipality, you may need a moving-out procedure at your old office. Address change If moving within the same municipality, the procedure may be called an address change or moving within the city. Residence Card update The new address may be written or updated on your Residence Card. title: The Bank & Phone Loop in Japan
summary: In Japan, opening a bank account and getting a mobile phone number can feel like a loop. A bank may ask for a phone number, while a phone company may ask for a bank account or Japanese payment method.
keywords: The Bank & Phone Loop in Japan | Japan Life Explained 🐾 Japan Life Explained Articles About FAQ Official resources Sources Disclaimer Daily life The Bank & Phone Loop in Japan In Japan, opening a bank account and getting a mobile phone number can feel like a loop. A bank may ask for a phone number, while a phone company may ask for a bank account or Japanese payment method. Quick answer The loop happens because banks, phone companies, and payment services all want a way to confirm your identity, contact you, and collect payment. The easiest way out is usually to prepare your Residence Card, registered address, contact method, and payment option before applying. Simple diagram Bank account may ask for phone number → Phone contract may ask for bank account or card → Payment setup may need both This is why people sometimes feel stuck: each service asks for something from another service. Why this happens For many services in Japan, companies need to know where you live, how to contact you, and how you will pay. If one of those pieces is missing, the application may become harder. Banks may want a Japanese phone number so they can contact you or verify your application. Mobile phone companies may want a bank account, credit card, or payment method. Online services may ask for SMS verification, which requires a usable phone number. Some applications may be harder if your address is not registered yet. A practical order that may help 1. Register your address Go to your city or ward office after moving in and register your address on your Residence Card. 2. Prepare a contact method If possible, use a temporary phone plan, prepaid SIM, eSIM, or contact number accepted by the service. 3. Try a bank that accepts your situation Some banks are easier for new residents, students, or employees than others. 4. Set up phone payment Once you have a bank account or accepted card, phone contracts and online services become easier. Some services may accept a credit card, prepaid option, or another payment method. “All banks have the same rules.” No. Requirements can di

title: How to Visit a Clinic in Japan
summary: Visiting a clinic in Japan is usually organized and practical, but the reception process, insurance check, and prescription system can feel confusing the first time.
keywords: How to Visit a Clinic in Japan | Japan Life Explained 🐾 Japan Life Explained Articles About FAQ Official resources Sources Disclaimer Health & Insurance How to Visit a Clinic in Japan Visiting a clinic in Japan is usually organized and practical, but the reception process, insurance check, and prescription system can feel confusing the first time. Quick answer When visiting a clinic in Japan, bring your health insurance proof, Residence Card, cash or payment method, and any medication information. At reception, you may fill out a medical questionnaire, wait, see the doctor, pay at the counter, and then take your prescription to a pharmacy. Simple diagram Reception show insurance proof → Questionnaire symptoms and history → Doctor visit examination → Payment & prescription clinic then pharmacy Before you go Many clinics accept walk-ins, but some require reservations. It is safer to check the clinic website or call first if you can. Some clinics have specific hours for first-time patients. Check the department For example: internal medicine, dermatology, ophthalmology, dentistry, orthopedics, or ENT. Check clinic hours Many clinics close during lunch and may have different hours by day. Bring documents Insurance proof, Residence Card, and medication information are useful. Prepare simple symptom words Writing symptoms on your phone can help if Japanese is difficult. What to bring Health insurance card, My Number health insurance card, or eligibility document Residence Card Cash or payment method Medication notebook, if you have one List or photo of medicines you currently take Referral letter, if another doctor gave you one Your address and phone number At reception At the clinic reception desk, staff usually check your insurance information and ask whether it is your first visit. You may receive a medical questionnaire called 問診票 . The questionnaire may ask about your symptoms, allergies, current medicines, medical history, pregnancy status, and emergency contact. After seeing the doctor After the examination, you usually return to the waiting area and wait for payment. If medicine is needed, the clinic may give you a prescription. In many cases, you take the prescription to a pharmacy nearby. 🐱 Cat tip: In Japan, the clinic and pharmacy are often separate. Do not be surprised if you pay at the clinic and then pay again at the pharmacy. Important Japanese words Japanese Romaji English 病院 byōin hospital クリニック kurinikku clinic 受付 uketsuke reception 初診 shoshin

title: Guarantor Companies in Japan: What Renters Should Know
summary: When renting an apartment in Japan, you may be asked to use a guarantor company. This can be confusing because it feels like another fee on top of rent, deposit, and key money.
keywords: Guarantor Companies in Japan: What Renters Should Know | Japan Life Explained 🐾 Japan Life Explained About FAQ Articles Official resources Request a topic Disclaimer Housing Guarantor Companies in Japan: What Renters Should Know When renting an apartment in Japan, you may be asked to use a guarantor company. This can be confusing because it feels like another fee on top of rent, deposit, and key money. Quick answer A guarantor company is a company that helps guarantee rent payment to the landlord. Many rental contracts in Japan require one, even if you have a job or enough savings. The tenant usually pays a guarantor fee at the start and may also pay renewal fees. Simple diagram Tenant rents the apartment → Guarantor company guarantees payment → Landlord reduces payment risk Why guarantor companies are used Landlords want confidence that rent will be paid. In the past, many contracts required a personal guarantor, often a family member. Now many properties use guarantor companies instead. For foreign renters, this can be especially common because landlords may worry about language, visa status, length of stay, overseas emergency contacts, or whether rent can be collected if there is a problem. What the fee usually means The guarantor fee is not rent and is usually not a deposit. It is a fee paid to the guarantor company for providing the guarantee service. The fee structure depends on the company and property. Initial guarantor fee A fee paid when signing the contract. It may be based on rent or a fixed amount. Renewal fee Some guarantor companies charge a renewal fee every year or at contract renewal. Monthly fee Some contracts include a monthly guarantor or payment service fee. Late payment cost If rent is late, extra fees or collection procedures may apply. Check the contract carefully. title: Health Insurance in Japan: What New Residents Should Know
summary: Japan has public health insurance systems. If you live in Japan, you usually need to be enrolled in a health insurance system and show your insurance card or eligibility confirmation when visiting a clinic.
keywords: Health Insurance in Japan: What New Residents Should Know | Japan Life Explained 🐾 Japan Life Explained Articles About FAQ Official resources Sources Disclaimer Health & Insurance Health Insurance in Japan: What New Residents Should Know Japan has public health insurance systems. If you live in Japan, you usually need to be enrolled in a health insurance system and show your insurance card or eligibility confirmation when visiting a clinic. Quick answer Health insurance in Japan usually reduces the amount you pay at clinics and hospitals. Many working employees are covered through their employer. People who are not covered by employer insurance usually join National Health Insurance through their city or ward office. Simple diagram You live in Japan resident status and address → Join insurance employer or city office → Visit clinic show insurance proof Two common types Employee health insurance Many full-time employees and some part-time employees are enrolled through their employer. The insurance premium is usually deducted from salary. National Health Insurance People not covered by employee insurance often enroll at the city or ward office. Premiums are usually paid to the municipality. What insurance usually does When you use public health insurance at a covered clinic or hospital, you usually pay only part of the medical cost at the counter. The exact share can depend on age, income, and situation. Health insurance does not mean everything is free. You still usually pay a portion of the cost, and some services may not be covered. Insurance card, My Number card, and eligibility Japan is changing how people show health insurance eligibility. Some people use a My Number card registered for health insurance, while others may use other confirmation documents depending on their situation. 🐱 Cat tip: When visiting a clinic, bring your Residence Card and any health insurance proof you have, especially if you are not sure what the clinic accepts. title: Move-in Costs in Japan: Key Money, Deposit, and Fees
summary: Renting an apartment in Japan can require several upfront payments. The first bill may be much higher than one month of rent.
keywords: Move-in Costs in Japan: Key Money, Deposit, and Fees | Japan Life Explained 🐾 Japan Life Explained Articles About FAQ Official resources Sources Disclaimer Housing Move-in Costs in Japan: Key Money, Deposit, and Fees Renting an apartment in Japan can require several upfront payments. The first bill may be much higher than one month of rent. Quick answer Move-in costs in Japan may include deposit, key money, agency fee, guarantor company fee, first month rent, next month rent, insurance, lock exchange fee, cleaning fee, and other contract-related costs. Always ask for a written breakdown before signing. Simple diagram Monthly rent base rent + Initial fees deposit / key money / agency + Contract costs insurance / guarantor / cleaning Why the first payment is high In many countries, people expect to pay only rent and a deposit. In Japan, the initial payment can include several different fees, and some of them are not returned. This is why the first payment can feel surprisingly high. 🐱 Cat tip: Do not compare only monthly rent. Compare the total initial cost and the renewal cost too. Common move-in cost items Deposit Money kept as security for unpaid rent or damage. Some or all may be returned depending on the contract and move-out condition. Key money A non-refundable payment to the landlord. It is common in some rentals but not all. Agency fee A fee paid to the real estate agency for arranging the rental contract. Guarantor company fee A fee for a company that guarantees payment if the tenant does not pay rent. First month / next month rent You may pay rent for the current month and the next month upfront. Insurance and other fees Fire insurance, lock exchange, cleaning, support service, and other fees may appear. Important Japanese words Japanese Romaji English 家賃 yachin rent 敷金 shikikin deposit 礼金 reikin key money 仲介手数料 chūkai tesūryō agency fee 保証会社 hoshō gaisha guarantor company 保証料 hoshōryō guarantor fee 火災保険 kasai hoken fire insurance 鍵交換費用 kagi kōkan hiyō lock exchange fee クリーニング費用 kurīningu hiyō cleaning fee 更新料 kōshinryō renewal fee Example: why “one month rent” is not enough This is a simple example, not a rule. Actual costs depend on the property and contract. title: How to Read a Japanese Payslip
summary: A Japanese payslip can look intimidating because it contains many short words and deduction names. Start by separating it into earnings, deductions, and final payment.
keywords: How to Read a Japanese Payslip | Japan Life Explained 🐾 Japan Life Explained Articles About FAQ Official resources Sources Disclaimer Salary & Tax How to Read a Japanese Payslip A Japanese payslip can look intimidating because it contains many short words and deduction names. Start by separating it into earnings, deductions, and final payment. Quick answer To read a Japanese payslip, first find the total earnings, then find the total deductions, and finally find the amount actually paid. The final payment may be written as 差引支給額 or another similar term depending on the company system. Simple diagram 支給 earnings / payments − 控除 deductions ＝ 差引支給額 final paid amount The three main areas 1. Earnings area This shows salary, overtime pay, commuting allowance, and other payments. 2. Deductions area This shows taxes, health insurance, pension, employment insurance, and other deductions. 3. Final payment area This is the amount transferred to your bank account. Common earnings items Basic salary Your regular base salary before overtime or extra allowances. Overtime pay Additional pay for overtime work, if applicable. Commuting allowance Money paid for commuting costs. It may appear as a separate item. Other allowances Depending on the company, there may be housing, family, qualification, or position allowances. Common deduction items 所得税 Income tax withheld from your salary. 住民税 Resident tax. It may start later depending on your situation. 健康保険 Health insurance deduction. 厚生年金 Employees' pension insurance deduction. 雇用保険 Employment insurance deduction. その他控除 Other deductions, such as company-specific fees, if applicable. Important Japanese words Japanese Romaji English 給与明細 kyūyo meisai payslip 支給 shikyū payment / earnings 控除 kōjo deduction 基本給 kihonkyū base salary 残業代 zangyōdai overtime pay 通勤手当 tsūkin teate commuting allowance 総支給額 sō shikyūgaku total earnings 控除合計 kōjo gōkei total deductions 差引支給額 sashihiki shikyūgaku final payment after deductions 振込額 furikomi gaku bank transfer amount How to check your payslip step by step Step 1: Find total earnings Look for words like 総支給額 or 支給合計. Step 2: Find total deductions Look for 控除合計 or the list of tax and insurance deductions. Step 3: Find final payment Look for 差引支給額, 振込額, or a similar final amount. Step 4: Compare with your bank deposit The final payment should usually match the amount transferred to your bank account. Common misunderstandings “All numbers are money I will receive.” No. Some numbers are earnings,

title: Resident Tax in Japan: Why It Often Starts Later
summary: Resident tax is one of the most confusing salary deductions in Japan because it is usually based on your previous year’s income and paid to your local municipality.
keywords: Resident Tax in Japan: Why It Often Starts Later | Japan Life Explained 🐾 Japan Life Explained Articles About FAQ Official resources Sources Disclaimer Salary & Tax Resident Tax in Japan: Why It Often Starts Later Resident tax is one of the most confusing salary deductions in Japan because it is usually based on your previous year’s income and paid to your local municipality. Quick answer Resident tax is a local tax paid to the city, ward, town, or village where you live. It is often calculated based on your income from the previous year. Because of this timing, people who start working in Japan may not see resident tax immediately, but may start paying it later. Simple diagram Last year’s income basis for calculation → Municipality calculates city/ward/town/village → This year’s payments salary deduction or bills Why it feels delayed Resident tax often does not feel like an immediate tax. That is because the tax amount is usually calculated after the year ends, based on income information from the previous year. This creates a timing gap: Year 1 You earn income in Japan. After Year 1 Your income information is reported and processed. Year 2 Resident tax based on Year 1 income may start being collected. How resident tax is paid Special collection Your employer deducts resident tax from your salary and pays it to the municipality. This is common for employees. Ordinary collection You receive tax bills from the municipality and pay them yourself. This may happen in some situations. The method can depend on your employment status, timing, and municipality procedures. Why your take-home pay may drop If resident tax was not deducted during your first months or first year, your take-home pay may feel higher at first. Later, when resident tax starts, your monthly take-home pay may become lower even if your gross salary did not change. 🐱 Cat tip: A lower second-year take-home pay does not always mean your salary went down. Resident tax may have started. Important Japanese words Japanese Romaji English 住民税 jūminzei resident tax 市区町村 shikuchōson city / ward / town / village 自治体 jichitai local municipality 前年所得 zennen shotoku previous-year income 特別徴収 tokubetsu chōshū special collection through salary 普通徴収 futsū chōshū ordinary collection by bills 納付書 nōfusho payment slip 給与天引き kyūyo tenbiki salary deduction Common misunderstandings “Resident tax is the same as income tax.” No. Income tax is national. Resident tax is local and paid to your municipality. “If I did not

title: Why Is My Take-home Pay Lower Than My Salary in Japan?
summary: Your salary offer or contract usually shows gross salary. The money that arrives in your bank account is take-home pay after taxes and social insurance deductions.
keywords: Why Is My Take-home Pay Lower Than My Salary in Japan? | Japan Life Explained 🐾 Japan Life Explained Articles About FAQ Official resources Sources Disclaimer Salary & Tax Why Is My Take-home Pay Lower Than My Salary in Japan? Your salary offer or contract usually shows gross salary. The money that arrives in your bank account is take-home pay after taxes and social insurance deductions. Quick answer In Japan, your monthly salary usually has several deductions before you receive it. Common deductions include income tax, resident tax, health insurance, pension, employment insurance, and sometimes company-specific items. Your bank transfer amount is the final amount after those deductions. Simple diagram Gross salary amount before deductions → Taxes & insurance deducted from salary → Take-home pay amount paid to you The basic idea When people first work in Japan, the most surprising thing is often that the salary amount written in the contract is not the amount paid into the bank account. This is normal. The contract amount is usually gross salary , and the transfer amount is net pay or take-home pay . Think of your salary in three layers: 1. Earnings Base salary, overtime pay, commuting allowance, and other payments. 2. Deductions Taxes, social insurance, pension, and other amounts subtracted. 3. Final payment The amount actually transferred to your bank account. Common deductions Income tax A national tax withheld from your salary. The amount depends on income and other conditions. Resident tax A local tax paid to your municipality. It often starts later, so it can surprise people in their second year. Health insurance Insurance that helps reduce medical costs when you visit clinics or hospitals. Pension Public pension contribution. It is often one of the larger monthly deductions. Employment insurance A smaller deduction related to unemployment and employment support programs. Company-specific items Some companies may deduct cafeteria fees, union fees, housing, or other items if applicable. Why resident tax surprises people Resident tax is often based on your income from the previous year. Because of that, some new workers may not see much resident tax at first, but it may appear later. This timing is one of the most common surprises for foreign residents and new employees. 🐱 Cat tip: If your second-year take-home pay suddenly feels lower, resident tax may be one reason. title: About Japan Life Explained
summary: Japan Life Explained is a simple guide site for people who are working, living, or preparing to live in Japan.
keywords: About Japan Life Explained | Japan Life Explained 🐾 Japan Life Explained Articles Official resources Request a topic Disclaimer About this site About Japan Life Explained Japan Life Explained is a simple guide site for people who are working, living, or preparing to live in Japan. What this site does This site explains everyday systems in Japan in plain English: salary, taxes, health insurance, clinics, address registration, renting, banking, phones, and other practical topics. The goal is simple: make confusing Japanese procedures easier to understand before you are standing at a counter, holding a form, and wondering what to do next. Who this site is for New residents People who recently moved to Japan and need to handle city office, health insurance, banking, phone, and housing procedures. Workers in Japan People who want to understand payslips, take-home pay, resident tax, social insurance, and salary deductions. Students and job seekers People preparing for life in Japan and trying to understand what documents and procedures may come next. People helping foreign residents Employers, friends, family members, and support staff who want simple English explanations to share. How the guides are written Plain English first The guides avoid unnecessary legal or bureaucratic language whenever possible. Japanese words included Important terms are shown in Japanese, romaji, and English so you can recognize them on forms or signs. Practical flow Each guide focuses on what people actually need to do, bring, ask, or check. Common misunderstandings Many problems come from assumptions, so each guide highlights easy-to-miss points. What this site is not This site is not a government website, law office, tax office, immigration office, city office, hospital, bank, or real estate agency. It does not replace official instructions or professional advice. Rules can differ depending on your visa status, city, employer, school, bank, phone company, clinic, landlord, and personal situ

title: Frequently Asked Questions
summary: A quick explanation of what Japan Life Explained is, how to use it, and what it cannot replace.
keywords: FAQ | Japan Life Explained 🐾 Japan Life Explained About FAQ Articles Official resources Request a topic Disclaimer FAQ Frequently Asked Questions A quick explanation of what Japan Life Explained is, how to use it, and what it cannot replace. Quick answer Use this site to understand the basic idea and common steps. Then check official sources or ask the relevant office, company, school, clinic, bank, or real estate agency before making important decisions. Is this an official Japanese government website? No. Japan Life Explained is an independent guide site. It explains common procedures in plain English, but official offices and professional advisers should be used for final decisions. Can I rely on this site for legal, tax, medical, or immigration advice? No. This site is a general starting point. Rules can differ by city, employer, school, visa status, bank, clinic, and personal situation. Who is this site for? It is for people who are working, living, studying, or preparing to live in Japan, especially people who find official Japanese procedures difficult to understand. Why do the guides include Japanese and romaji? Because many forms, counters, payslips, clinic documents, and rental papers use Japanese terms. Romaji helps readers say or recognize the words. Why are the articles simple? The goal is not to cover every exception. The goal is to help readers understand the basic idea, common mistakes, and next step before checking official details. What should I do if my situation is different? Check official sources and ask the relevant office, company, school, employer, clinic, bank, or real estate agency directly. Can I request a new topic? Yes. Use the Request a topic page to suggest topics that would help foreign residents in Japan. Why does the site use cat tips? The cat tips are small reminders that make stressful procedures feel a little less intimidating. 🐱 Cat tip: If a procedure feels confusing, first identify the office, document, deadline, and next act
